coredata.dc:title: Computational insights into the tailoring of photoelectric properties in graphene quantum dot-Ru(II) polypyridyl nanocomposites

coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dot-Ru(II) polypyridyl nanocomposites have emerged as promising materials for photoelectric applications due to their unique optoelectronic properties. This study investigates the impact of pyrenyl, 2,8-di-tert-butylpyreno[4,5-b:9,10-b’]dithiophene, and 2,8-di-tert-butyl-4,10-dihydropyrrolo[3′,2′:9,10]phenanthro[4,5-efg]indole substituents and N^N or C^N analogues of dipyrido[3,2-α:2′,3′-c]phenazine (dppz) on the photophysical characteristics and photoelectric behavior of Ru(II) complexes and their nanocomposites using density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The findings reveal that the incorporation of these substituents and the choice of ligand system significantly influence the chemical reactivity, electron injection, and ground state regeneration processes of the nanocomposites. The C^N nanocomposites demonstrate superior energy conversion efficiencies (14.9–15.6%) compared to the N^N counterparts (1.49–13.4%) due to their higher open-circuit voltages and fill factors. The pyrenyl substituent enhances light absorption and photocurrent generation in the N^N-based nanocomposite but slightly reduces efficiency in the C^N-based nanocomposite. The nanocomposites exhibit improved nonlinear optical characteristics compared to the individual Ru(II) complexes, with the N^N-based nanocomposites showing remarkably higher total hyperpolarizability values. These findings provide valuable insights for designing advanced materials tailored for photoelectric applications by strategically modifying the structural components of GQD-Ru(II) polypyridyl nanocomposites.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)-(d) Schematic structures of GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites. The pyrenyl substituent is attached to the (b) dppz or (d) C^N analogue of dppz of Ru(II) complex. (e) R1 and R2 are three heteroaryl-fused di-tert-butyl substituted ligands.
Projected density of states (PDOS) of GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites.

A visual representation is provided to demonstrate the transfer of charge at the interface between GQD and Ru(II) complex. The Ru(II) complex controls the HOMO and LUMO in the C^N-based nanocomposite. Conversely, in the N^N-based nanocomposite, the GQD controls the HOMO while the Ru(II) complex governs the LUMO.

(a)-(h) The absorption spectra of GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites were calculated and are represented by the red and blue lines. The absorption spectrum of isolated GQD is depicted by the red line, whereas the absorption spectrum of the Ru(II) complex is shown by the blue line. The spectra of isolated GQD and Ru(II) complex were directly extracted from the optimized geometry of the nanocomposite without any additional optimization.
Optoelectronic and reactivity parameters of isolated Ru(II) complexes and their nanocomposites.
Illustrations of frontier molecular orbital (FMO) for Ru(II) complex and their nanocomposites.

Both isolated Ru(II) complexes and their nanocomposites were studied for the free energy changes related to electron injection (ΔGinj) and ground state regeneration (ΔGreg), along with the charge transfer distance (ΔL) and interfragment charge transfer (ΔQ).
Energy conversion efficiency (Ω) and photovoltaic parameters for GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposite.
Total hyperpolarizability (κtot) of isolated Ru(II) complexes and GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: Computational insights into the tailoring of photoelectric properties in graphene quantum dot-Ru(II) polypyridyl nanocomposites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dot-Ru(II) polypyridyl nanocomposites have emerged as promising materials for photoelectric applications due to their unique optoelectronic properties. This study investigates the impact of pyrenyl, 2,8-di-tert-butylpyreno[4,5-b:9,10-b’]dithiophene, and 2,8-di-tert-butyl-4,10-dihydropyrrolo[3′,2′:9,10]phenanthro[4,5-efg]indole substituents and N^N or C^N analogues of dipyrido[3,2-α:2′,3′-c]phenazine (dppz) on the photophysical characteristics and photoelectric behavior of Ru(II) complexes and their nanocomposites using density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The findings reveal that the incorporation of these substituents and the choice of ligand system significantly influence the chemical reactivity, electron injection, and ground state regeneration processes of the nanocomposites. The C^N nanocomposites demonstrate superior energy conversion efficiencies (14.9–15.6%) compared to the N^N counterparts (1.49–13.4%) due to their higher open-circuit voltages and fill factors. The pyrenyl substituent enhances light absorption and photocurrent generation in the N^N-based nanocomposite but slightly reduces efficiency in the C^N-based nanocomposite. The nanocomposites exhibit improved nonlinear optical characteristics compared to the individual Ru(II) complexes, with the N^N-based nanocomposites showing remarkably higher total hyperpolarizability values. These findings provide valuable insights for designing advanced materials tailored for photoelectric applications by strategically modifying the structural components of GQD-Ru(II) polypyridyl nanocomposites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Significant progress has been achieved in photoelectric applications through the exploration of new materials and molecular complexes with impressive electronic properties when exposed to light [1–4]. Among these, Ru(II)-polypyridyl complexes have garnered considerable attention due to their unique attributes in light absorption, electrochemical behavior, and photophysical properties. These complexes consist of a central ruthenium ion coordinated with various polypyridine ligands, allowing for precise tuning of their electronic and photonic responses. Ru(II)-polypyridyl complexes are particularly noted for their strong absorption in the visible spectrum, prolonged excited states, and ability to undergo light-induced electron transfer [5–7]. These properties make them highly attractive for applications such as solar energy conversion, light-emitting diodes, and sensor development [8,9]. Moreover, their versatility and robust coordination chemistry enable the creation of derivatives with tailored functionalities. In the realm of photodynamic therapy (PDT), these compounds have shown promise as effective agents due to their ability to generate harmful reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon light exposure [8–11]. This characteristic allows for the targeted destruction of cancer cells while minimizing damage to surrounding healthy tissues. Additionally, the extensive photophysical properties of Ru(II)-polypyridyl complexes offer potential for use in organellar imaging and tumor detection, further expanding their applications in biomedicine [10,12,13].

Manipulating the characteristics of excited states in Ru(II)-polypyridyl complexes is essential for photoelectric devices. By enhancing the lifetime of these excited states and improving the quantum yields of electron transfer processes, it becomes possible to create more efficient devices. Subtle modifications to the ligands can result in different characteristics of the excited states, offering the opportunity to adjust their photophysical properties and enhance device performance. Fredin et al. conducted a study exploring the excited state spectrum of three ruthenium complexes with non-polypyridine ligands, demonstrating the diverse photochemical properties of these ligands and contrasting them with conventional polypyridyl complexes. In the latter complexes, the exchange of ligands is made easier by elongating the bonds between the metal and ligands in order to support a new coordination arrangement [14]. Dong et al. investigated how electron-donor ancillary ligands can improve the optical properties of heteroleptic Ru(II)-polypyridyl complexes in dye-sensitized solar cells. They discovered that these ligands improve light harvesting and energy alignment [15]. Romanova et al. concentrated on altering the characteristics of Ru(II)-polypyridyl complexes in their excited state for use in photoelectric devices. They achieved this by designing complexes that exhibit tunable single and dual optical emission through excited state design [16]. Lastly, Hua et al. created a Ru(II)-polypyridyl complex that targets DNA and has a durable intraligand excited state, demonstrating potential for use in photodynamic therapy [17]. It was found that slight changes to the ligands can alter the photo-physicochemical characteristics of Ru(II)-polypyridyl complexes. They created a Ru(II)-polypyridyl complex that targets DNA and exhibited greatly improved photobiological effectiveness. Peña et al. synthesized novel cyclometallated Ru(II) complexes and found that adding the 2-phenyl pyridine (phpy) ligand to the coordination sphere decreased the energy level of the metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) excited states and shortened their duration [18]. Lowering the energy of the 3MLCT state below that of [Ru(phpy)(bpy)2] significantly reduced the lifetime of the excited state. Recent research efforts have been focused on designing Ru(II)-polypyridyl complexes with intraligand excited states that have long lifetimes, with the aim of utilizing their full potential in photoelectric applications. As an example, Chen and colleagues investigated the second-order nonlinear optical characteristics of Ru(II) polypyridine compounds [19]. Utilizing DFT, they were able to examine how the molecular structure relates to the nonlinear optical properties of the compounds, offering important knowledge on their potential as materials for nonlinear optics. Kaufmann et al. investigated the photophysical properties of Ru(II)-polypyridyl complexes that contained thiazole π-extended dipyridophenazine ligands [20]. Their findings revealed that Ru(II) complexes with thiazole ligands exhibited dual emission with long lifetimes. Wei et al. also conducted research in this area. The extension of the Ru(II)-polypyridyl complex's longevity has been recorded by adding pyrene groups as methoxyl substitutes [21]. Yan et al. delves into the examination of the electronic and photophysical characteristics of a range of neutral Ru(II) complexes featuring a donor-metal-acceptor configuration. The primary focus of this exploration is to assess their suitability for utilization in light-harvesting, optoelectronic devices, and other relevant areas [22].

Nanoscale particles known as Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs), which are derived from graphene, exhibit remarkable electronic, optical, and chemical properties, including high conductivity, wide absorption range, and intense photoluminescence, along with biocompatibility and stability [23–26]. These properties make GQDs highly versatile for optoelectronics, sensing, and biomedical applications [27,28]. When GQDs are integrated with metal–organic complexes, known for their robust visible light absorption and efficient photo-induced electron transfer, the resulting composite benefits from enhanced light harvesting across a wider solar spectrum and improved charge carrier separation [29–31]. For example, Cui et al. examined how the π-conjugation in the cyclometalating ligands of the Ir(III) complexes influences the photoelectric conversion efficiency of the nanocomposites [31]. Mihalache et al. examines the movement of charges among various parts of the solar cell, such as the organic sensitizers, GQDs, and electron acceptors [29]. Moreover, the high surface area of GQDs combined with the catalytic activity of Ru(II) complexes can significantly boost photocatalytic processes. The study examined the influence of different substituents on the photocatalytic performance of bipyridyl Pt(II) bisstilbenylacetylide complexes when attached to GQDs [32]. Moreover, the study investigated how the efficiency of a nanocomposite made up of GQDs and an Ir(III) complex is affected by π-conjugation, with the goal of determining the influence of π-conjugation on the photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposite [30]. Furthermore, the functionalized iridium-doped graphene nanomaterials were studied for improved delivery of Piroxicam to gain insights into the interaction between the nanomaterials and Piroxicam, aiming to enhance drug delivery efficiency [33].

In this work, we performed DFT-based calculations to explore the optoelectronic and photoelectric properties of nanocomposites composed of GQDs and a Ru(II) complex. Integrating GQDs with Ru(II) complexes significantly enhances photoelectric applications. GQDs, renowned for their excellent electronic properties, complement the photophysical attributes of Ru(II) complexes, resulting in amplified performance. GQDs facilitate efficient charge separation and transfer upon photoexcitation, enhancing quantum efficiency in devices such as photovoltaic cells and photodetectors. Their adjustable characteristics due to size and surface modification make them perfect companions for diverse Ru(II) compounds, expanding light capture and facilitating customized photoresponsive substances. Prior research has indicated that adding a pyrenyl group to dipyrido[3,2-α:2′,3′-c]phenazine (dppz) allows for the formation of the low-energy, enduring pyrene-centered triplet intraligand (3IL) state in the resulting Ru(II) compound [17]. Additionally, we also constructed two other Ru(II) complexes that contain heteroatoms in the pyrene derivatives for comparison (Fig. 1 (e)), to understand the structure–property relationship. Compound R1, known as 2,8-di-tert-butylpyreno[4,5-b:9,10-b']dithiophene, features a pyrene core with two thiophene rings fused at the 4,5- and 9,10-positions, forming a polycyclic aromatic system. It has tert-butyl groups at the 2- and 8-positions to enhance solubility. The sulfur atoms in the thiophene rings influence electronic properties and conjugation while maintaining aromaticity, crucial for optoelectronic applications. Compound R2, 2,8-di-tert-butyl-4,10-dihydropyrrolo[3′,2′:9,10]phenanthro[4,5-efg]indole, also has a pyrene core but includes a pyrrole ring fused at the 4,10-positions and an indole moiety fused at the 3′,2′-positions, creating a complex polycyclic aromatic system. Like R1, it has tert-butyl groups at the 2- and 8-positions for enhanced solubility. The nitrogen atoms in the pyrrole and indole rings affect the electronic structure and reactivity while preserving the aromatic character essential for optoelectronic applications. To simplify, we replace the tert-butyl groups with hydrogen atoms to reduce computational costs. Patent applications have previously proposed or reported both compound R1 and R2 [57,58]. The photophysical characteristics of Ru(II) complexes are altered by these adjustments when paired with a diimine (N^N) or cyclometalated (C^N) version of dppz, as shown in Fig. 1. The effects of pyrenyl, R1 and R2 substituents, and N^N or C^N analogues of dppz on the photophysical properties and photoelectric performance of Ru(II) complexes and their nanocomposites remain unknown and require further research to determine. It is essential to comprehend how these factors impact the design of nanocomposite materials in order to enhance their performance in applications like solar cells, sensors, and light-emitting devices.

This study aimed to analyze the optoelectronic and photoelectric characteristics of nanocomposites that include a GQD and a Ru(II) complex, namely the GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposite, through the use of DFT calculations. In Fig. 1, the Ru(II) complexes were simulated with a pyrenyl group connected to either the N^N or C^N version of dppz. The Ru(II) complex is labeled as Ru(II)-N^N-p when the pyrenyl substituent is attached to the N^N analogue, and as Ru(II)-C^N-p when it is connected to the C^N analogue. Similarly, when the R1 or R2 group is attached to the N^N analogue, the complex is labeled as Ru(II)-N^N-R1 or Ru(II)-N^N-R2, respectively. When these groups are attached to the C^N analogue, the complex is labeled as Ru(II)-C^N-R1 or Ru(II)-C^N-R2, respectively. Ru(II) complexes lacking pyrenyl substituents are referred to as Ru(II)-N^N and Ru(II)-C^N, respectively. The GQD is connected to Ru(II) complex via amidation bond. Through extensive DFT calculations, Li et al. examined different absorption modes related to amidation bonding and compared them to radical addition and cycloaddition of an organic molecule with GQDs [34]. Their thorough analysis unequivocally demonstrates that amidation bonds surpass the other two methods in terms of photoconversion efficiency. Thus, eight nanocomposites of GQD-Ru(II) have been developed, specifically GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p, GQD/Ru(II)-N^N, GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R1, GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R2, GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-p, GQD/Ru(II)-C^N, GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R1 and GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R2. The initial configurations of the Ru(II) complexes and GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites were optimized without symmetry constraints using the scalar relativistic sf-X2C Hamiltonian and the PBE0 hybrid functional, with strict convergence criteria on energies and gradients applied in the BDF software package [35–38]. For non-metal atoms like C, N, O, H, S, a double-zeta quality basis set x2c-SVPall [39,40] was used. This offers a balance of accuracy and computational cost. For the Ru metal center, the more flexible triple-zeta x2c-TZVPall [39–41] basis was used to better describe the metal's electronic structure. Time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) calculations were performed to determine vertical excitation energies and oscillator strengths for dichloromethane, incorporating a polarizable continuum solvent model [46,64]. The Multiwfn software package [43] was used to calculate parameters such as the distance of charge transfer (ΔL) and the amount of transferred charges (ΔQ) between GQD and Ru(II) complex in order to understand interfacial charge transfer.

Additional calculations validated the variation of computed bandgaps with respect to basis set. Various sizes of basis sets were experimented with, including changing the basis set for Ru from x2c-TZVPall to x2c-QZVPall and adjusting the basis set for non-metal elements from x2c-SVall to x2c-SVPall-2c, as illustrated in supplemental Figure S1. The results demonstrated that varying the basis set sizes has a negligible effect on the calculated bandgaps of the GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites. In a previous investigation [44], the accuracy of different functionals in calculating the bandgaps of pristine GQD was evaluated. The examination encompassed five distinct functionals: PBE, B3LYP, CAM-B3LYP, PBE0, and wb97x. The bandgap values obtained from these functionals ranged from 2.82 eV to 7.67 eV. Notably, the incorporation of a greater level of exact exchange in CAM-B3LYP and wb97x yielded considerably larger bandgaps when compared to PBE0 and B3LYP. The last two functionals included moderate amounts of precise exchange and resulted in bandgaps that closely matched other computational findings and tight-binding calculations. The previous study has shown that the PBE0 functional is reliable in accurately predicting the excitation characteristics of materials based on graphene. Furthermore, using the PBE0 functional with the x2c-TZVPall/x2c-SVall mixed basis set, we computed the absorption spectra of two Ru(II) complexes (Supplemental Figure S2). The Ru(II)-N^N complex's absorption spectra showed a red-shift compared to the Ru(II)-C^N complex, aligning with experimental evidence [17]. Additionally, the major absorption peaks in both the Ru(II)-C^N and Ru(II)-N^N complexes closely aligned with the experimental spectra, confirming the appropriateness of the selected functional and basis set for characterizing the electronic structures and excited-state properties of the studied Ru(II) complexes. Overall, the PBE0 method accurately describes the optical and electronic characteristics of nanocomposites containing GQDs and Ru(II) complexes.

Comprehending the importance of chemical reactivity, including chemical hardness, chemical potential, and electron-donating ability, is crucial for understanding and forecasting the photoelectric properties of nanomaterials. Chemical hardness (η) delineates a material's resistance to electron density alterations under external perturbations, crucial for maintaining nanomaterial stability and preserving photoelectric properties over time. Chemical potential (μ) dictates the movement of charge carriers within nanomaterials, impacting charge separation and transport processes essential for photoelectric phenomena. The promotion of effective charge transfer in devices like solar cells and photodetectors is made possible by the impact of electron-donating power (ω-) on the process of charge transfer and recombination at interfaces. Nanomaterials with lower values of μ and ω- exhibit exceptional capabilities in accepting and donating electrons. To delve further into the calculation of η, μ, and ω-, please consult the provided references [45,46].

Effective communication between dye sensitizers and the TiO2 semiconductor surface is essential for maximizing the efficiency of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). Therefore, the meticulous choice of the TiO2 surface holds great significance. The (101) surface of bulk anatase TiO2, with its exceptionally low surface energy, is the preferred surface for dye adsorption [47,48]. Fig. 1 portrays the attachment of GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites to the (TiO2)38 cluster [30,49], representing the surface of the semiconductor. This enables a comprehensive examination of the charge transfer mechanism between dye sensitizers and TiO2, thanks to the successful capture of TiO2′s intricate properties using small cluster models. The effectiveness of this configuration is further confirmed through experimental verification [50]. The Ru(II) complexes attach to the (TiO2)38 surface, acting as the location for attachment. The use of the bidentate chelating mode ensures a dependable and long-lasting interaction. The calculated lengths of the Ti-O bonds range from 2.16 to 2.19 Ã… in the molecular systems, which is consistent with earlier research [51–53]. Utilizing this comprehensive strategy enhances understanding of the dynamics of charge transfer and the structure of hybrid systems, ultimately advancing the development of efficient DSSCs.

The data in Table 1 reveals that incorporating a pyrenyl group decreases the chemical hardness of the C^N system (Ru(II)-C^N-p: 2.51 eV) compared to the unsubstituted counterparts (Ru(II)-C^N: 2.65 eV). This decrease can be attributed to the highly conjugated nature of the pyrenyl group, which extends the π-system of the C^N ligand. This extension facilitates greater delocalization of electron density across the molecule, thereby lowering the HOMO-LUMO gap [60]. A smaller HOMO-LUMO gap corresponds to lower chemical hardness. Additionally, the pyrenyl group acts as an electron-donating moiety, destabilizing the HOMO, further reducing the energy gap between these orbitals and thus decreasing chemical hardness. Conversely, in the N^N system, incorporating the pyrenyl group increases the chemical hardness (Ru(II)-N^N-p: 2.61 eV vs. Ru(II)-N^N: 2.59 eV). This increase can be explained by the more electron-withdrawing nature of the N^N ligands due to the presence of nitrogen atoms. When the pyrenyl group is attached, it may interact differently with the electron-deficient N^N system. Unlike the C^N system, the electron-withdrawing nature of the N^N ligands might compete with the electron-donating properties of the pyrenyl group, limiting the extent of electron delocalization and leading to a less pronounced reduction in the HOMO-LUMO gap. In some cases, this interaction might even slightly increase the gap, thus increasing chemical hardness. Furthermore, the pyrenyl group might engage in different electronic communication with the N^N ligand system, leading to the stabilization of both the HOMO and LUMO, but not enough to decrease chemical hardness. Instead, this interaction could slightly increase chemical hardness due to the specific electronic interactions between the pyrenyl group and the N^N ligands. The introduction of R1 and R2 groups, which are heteroatom-containing pyrene derivatives, also decreases the chemical hardness of the C^N-based complexes (Ru(II)-C^N-R1: 2.53 eV, Ru(II)-C^N-R2: 2.57 eV) and more significantly for the N^N-based complexes (Ru(II)-N^N-R1: 2.05 eV, Ru(II)-N^N-R2: 2.39 eV), suggesting an additional increase in chemical reactivity compared to the unsubstituted complexes. Furthermore, the cyclometalated C^N analogue exhibits a higher chemical hardness (Ru(II)-C^N: 2.65 eV) than the N^N analogue (Ru(II)-N^N: 2.59 eV), suggesting a lower chemical reactivity. This can be explained by the stronger metal–ligand bonding and more delocalized electron distribution in the cyclometalated structure. The C^N analogue also shows a slightly more negative chemical potential (Ru(II)-C^N: –3.90 eV) than the N^N analogue (Ru(II)-N^N: –3.89 eV), indicating a slightly increased electron-accepting ability. However, the C^N analogue exhibits a lower electron-donating power (Ru(II)-C^N: 5.14 eV) compared to the N^N analogue (Ru(II)-N^N: 5.18 eV), confirming the better electron-donating ability of the diimine N^N ligand over the cyclometalated C^N ligand.

The presence of the pyrenyl group makes the chemical potential more negative for both the C^N system (Ru(II)-C^N-p: –3.93 eV vs. Ru(II)-C^N: –3.90 eV) and the N^N system (Ru(II)-N^N-p: –3.98 eV vs. Ru(II)-N^N: –3.89 eV), indicating an enhanced electron-accepting ability. This effect can be attributed to several factors. Firstly, the pyrenyl group, being an electron-rich aromatic system, donates electron density to the molecule it is attached to, thereby increasing the overall electron density of the molecule. Additionally, the pyrenyl group stabilizes the LUMO, making it slightly lower in energy and facilitating the acceptance of additional electrons. Consequently, the molecule becomes more inclined to accept electrons, resulting in a more negative chemical potential. Moreover, the incorporation of R1 and R2 groups into the Ru(II) polypyridyl complexes generally leads to more negative chemical potentials for both C^N and N^N-based complexes, indicating an enhanced electron-accepting ability, except in the case of Ru(II)-C^N-R1. This variation can be attributed to the specific electronic effects and interactions of these substituents with the ligand systems. The R1 group features a pyrene core with two thiophene rings fused at the 4,5- and 9,10-positions. Thiophene rings contain sulfur atoms, which have notable electron-withdrawing properties due to their electronegativity. In Ru(II)-C^N-R1, the electron-withdrawing nature of the sulfur atoms competes with the electron-donating characteristics of the pyrene core. This competition can lead to a reduced overall electron density on the complex, thus making the chemical potential less negative compared to other substituted complexes. The electron-withdrawing sulfur atoms counterbalance the electron-donating effects of the pyrene core, resulting in a less pronounced enhancement of the electron-accepting ability. For N^N-based complexes, the electron-withdrawing nature of the thiophene rings in R1 can stabilize the LUMO, making the chemical potential more negative. The N^N ligands are already electron-withdrawing, and the addition of the sulfur atoms in R1 further enhances this effect, leading to a greater electron-accepting ability. The R2 group contains a pyrene core with a pyrrole ring fused at the 4,10-positions and an indole moiety fused at the 3′,2′-positions. Both pyrrole and indole rings contain nitrogen atoms, which can exhibit electron-donating properties through resonance. In Ru(II)-C^N-R2, the electron-donating nature of the nitrogen atoms in the pyrrole and indole rings increases the overall electron density on the complex, enhancing the electron-accepting ability by stabilizing the LUMO. This results in a more negative chemical potential. For N^N-based complexes, the electron-donating effects of the nitrogen atoms in R2 complement the electron-withdrawing nature of the N^N ligands. This synergy allows for better electron delocalization and stabilization of the LUMO, leading to a more negative chemical potential.

The pyrenyl group increases the electron-donating power in both C^N and N^N systems (Ru(II)-C^N-p: 5.35 eV, Ru(II)-N^N-p: 5.34 eV) compared to the unsubstituted counterparts (Ru(II)-C^N: 5.14 eV, Ru(II)-N^N: 5.18 eV), indicating a reduction in the electron-donating capacity of the complexes. The introduction of the R1 group decreases the electron-donating power of the C^N-based complexes (Ru(II)-C^N-R1: 5.11 eV), while the introduction of the R2 group increases the electron-donating power of the C^N-based complexes (Ru(II)-C^N-R2: 5.30 eV). The thiophene rings of R1 group contain sulfur atoms, which are moderately electron-withdrawing due to their electronegativity and inductive effect. The electron-withdrawing nature of the sulfur atoms in the thiophene rings reduces the electron density on the C^N ligand system, thereby decreasing its electron-donating power (Ru(II)-C^N-R1: 5.11 eV). Conversely, the R2 group contains a pyrene core with a pyrrole ring fused at the 4,10-positions and an indole moiety fused at the 3′,2′-positions. Both pyrrole and indole rings contain nitrogen atoms that are less electron-withdrawing than sulfur and can even exhibit electron-donating characteristics through resonance. The electron-donating nature of the nitrogen atoms in the pyrrole and indole rings increases the electron density on the C^N ligand system, thereby enhancing its electron-donating power (Ru(II)-C^N-R2: 5.30 eV). On the other hand, both R1 and R2 groups significantly increase the electron-donating power for the N^N-based complexes (Ru(II)-N^N-R1: 6.75 eV, Ru(II)-N^N-R2: 6.78 eV). This substantial increase is because the electron-rich pyrene core and the additional heteroatoms (sulfur in R1 and nitrogen in R2) interact favorably with the N^N ligand system. The nitrogen atoms in the N^N system can delocalize the electron density more effectively when coupled with the electron-donating effects of the R1 and R2 groups, leading to a significant increase in the overall electron-donating power.

The attachment of GQD to the Ru(II) complex affects the chemical hardness, chemical potential, and electron-donating power in different ways, depending on the specific complex and the type of ligand system (C^N or N^N). For GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-p, the attachment of GQD results in an increase in chemical hardness compared to the isolated counterpart, suggesting a decrease in chemical reactivity. In contrast, GQD/Ru(II)-C^N, GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R1 and GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R2 exhibit a decrease in chemical hardness upon GQD attachment, indicating an increase in chemical reactivity. For the N^N-based systems, the attachment of GQD leads to an increase in chemical hardness for GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R1, while it decreases for GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p, GQD/Ru(II)-N^N, and GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R2, suggesting a complex relationship between GQD attachment and chemical reactivity in these systems. The varying effects of GQD attachment on the chemical hardness of Ru(II)-polypyridyl complexes can be attributed to the complex interplay between the electronic properties of the GQD, the Ru(II) center, and the specific ligand system (C^N or N^N). For GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-p, this could be due to the strong electronic coupling between the GQD and the pyrenyl-substituted C^N ligand, which may stabilize the frontier molecular orbitals and increase the HOMO-LUMO gap. The increased chemical hardness indicates that the GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-p nanocomposite is less prone to electron transfer and chemical reactions compared to the isolated Ru(II)-C^N-p complex. In contrast, GQD/Ru(II)-C^N, GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R1, and GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R2 exhibit a decrease in chemical hardness upon GQD attachment, indicating an increase in chemical reactivity. This suggests that the electronic coupling between the GQD and the unsubstituted or R1/R2-substituted C^N ligands may destabilize the frontier molecular orbitals and decrease the HOMO-LUMO gap. The reduced chemical hardness implies that these nanocomposites are more susceptible to electron transfer and chemical reactions compared to their isolated counterparts. For the N^N-based systems, the attachment of GQD leads to an increase in chemical hardness for GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R1, while it decreases for GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p, GQD/Ru(II)-N^N, and GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R2. The increased chemical hardness of GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R1 suggests that the electronic coupling between the GQD and the R1-substituted N^N ligand may stabilize the frontier molecular orbitals and increase the HOMO-LUMO gap, resulting in a decrease in chemical reactivity. On the other hand, the decreased chemical hardness of GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p, GQD/Ru(II)-N^N, and GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R2 indicates that the electronic coupling between the GQD and the pyrenyl-substituted, unsubstituted, or R2-substituted N^N ligands may destabilize the frontier molecular orbitals and decrease the HOMO-LUMO gap, leading to an increase in chemical reactivity.

The chemical potential of the C^N-based complexes becomes more negative upon GQD attachment for Ru(II)-C^N-p, Ru(II)-C^N-R1, and Ru(II)-C^N-R2, indicating an increased tendency to accept electrons. This can be explained by the extended π-conjugation facilitated by the GQD attachment, which enhances electron delocalization and stabilizes the LUMO. The electron-rich nature of GQD also contributes to this effect by donating electron density to the attached complex, increasing the overall electron density and stabilizing the LUMO. Additionally, the presence of electron-donating substituents like pyrenyl in Ru(II)-C^N-p, thiophene in Ru(II)-C^N-R1, and pyrrole/indole in Ru(II)-C^N-R2 further enhances these effects, leading to a more negative chemical potential. Conversely, for Ru(II)-C^N, the chemical potential becomes slightly less negative upon GQD attachment, suggesting a reduced electron-accepting ability. This can be attributed to the lack of strong electron-donating substituents in Ru(II)-C^N, which means that the GQD attachment does not significantly enhance electron delocalization or stabilization of the LUMO. The interaction between Ru(II)-C^N and GQD might also be weaker compared to the other substituted complexes, resulting in a less pronounced stabilization of the LUMO and a slightly less negative chemical potential. For N^N-based complexes, the chemical potential becomes more negative upon GQD attachment except Ru(II)-N^N-R1, indicating an increased tendency to accept electrons. When a GQD is attached to an N^N-based Ru(II) complex, it donates electron density to the complex. This donation increases the overall electron density of the Ru(II) complex, which can lower the energy level of the LUMO and make the complex more stable and favorable for electron acceptance. The increased electron density provided by the GQD stabilizes the LUMO, making it lower in energy. This stabilization enhances the complex's ability to accept electrons, resulting in a more negative chemical potential. The GQD's electron-donating effect, combined with the electron-withdrawing nature of the N^N ligands, creates a synergistic effect that further increases the complex's electron-accepting ability. In Ru(II)-N^N-R1, the electron-withdrawing nature of the sulfur atoms in the thiophene rings competes with the electron donation from the GQD. This competition between the electron-withdrawing sulfur atoms and the electron-donating GQD results in a less pronounced increase in electron density compared to other N^N-based complexes. The electron-withdrawing effect of the sulfur atoms reduces the overall electron density on the complex, counteracting the electron-donating effect of the GQD.

The positioning of the Ru(II) complexes and their nanocomposites' LUMO and HOMO levels in relation to the TiO2 conduction band (–4.0 eV [30,31]) and I-/I3- HOMO level (–4.6 eV [30,31]) is essential for electron injection and ground state regeneration in DSSCs. All the studied complexes exhibit LUMO levels higher than –4.0 eV, ensuring thermodynamically favorable conditions for electron injection. The alignment of the LUMO and HOMO levels of the Ru(II) complexes and their nanocomposites relative to the TiO2 conduction band (–4.0 eV) and I−/I3− HOMO level (–4.6 eV) is crucial for efficient electron injection and ground state regeneration in DSSCs. All the studied complexes exhibit LUMO levels higher than –4.0 eV, ensuring thermodynamically favorable conditions for electron injection. The Ru(II)-C^N and GQD/Ru(II)-C^N show the highest LUMO levels (–2.57 eV and –2.59 eV, respectively), indicating the strongest driving force for electron injection. The cyclometalated C^N ligand generally raises the LUMO level compared to the diimine N^N ligand, facilitating electron injection. The presence of the R1 group slightly increases the LUMO level of the C^N-based complex (Ru(II)-C^N-R1: –2.55 eV) compared to Ru(II)-C^N (–2.57 eV), suggesting a minor enhancement in the driving force for electron injection. In contrast, the R2 group significantly lowers the LUMO level of the N^N-based complex (Ru(II)-N^N-R2: –3.30 eV) compared to Ru(II)-N^N (–3.24 eV), indicating a reduced driving force for electron injection. The attachment of GQD leads to varying effects on the LUMO levels of the Ru(II) complexes, depending on the specific complex and the ligand system. For C^N-based system, the LUMO level slightly decreases compared to its isolated counterpart, suggesting a minor decrease in the driving force for electron injection. In contrast, the LUMO levels of N^N-based systems vary based on the specific ligand upon GQD attachment. In particular, the LUMO levels of Ru(II)-N^N and Ru(II)-N^N-p significantly decrease upon GQD attachment, while Ru(II)-N^N-R1 increases and Ru(II)-N^N-R2 slightly decreases upon GQD attachment.

Regarding ground state regeneration, all the Ru(II) complexes possess HOMO levels lower than –4.6 eV, ensuring thermodynamically favorable conditions. Among the isolated complexes, Ru(II)-N^N-R2 exhibits the most efficient ground state regeneration with the lowest HOMO level at –5.69 eV. In general, the N^N analogue tends to have lower HOMO levels than the C^N analogue, suggesting that the diimine N^N ligand could lead to more efficient ground state regeneration compared to the cyclometalated C^N ligand. When the pyrenyl substituent is introduced in isolated systems, it elevates the HOMO level, leading to a slight reduction in the driving force for ground state regeneration in comparison to the unsubstituted complexes. The R2 groups further lower the HOMO levels of both C^N and N^N-based complexes, which may lead to a higher driving force for ground state regeneration. The attachment of GQD typically lowers the HOMO levels of Ru(II)-polypyridyl complexes compared to those without GQD, indicating a stronger driving force for ground state regeneration. However, the HOMO levels of GQD/Ru(II)-C^N and GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R2 increase compared to their isolated counterparts, suggesting a decreased driving force for ground state regeneration in these nanocomposites. It is important to note that the HOMO levels of all the GQD-attached systems remain lower than the HOMO level of I−/I3− (–4.6 eV), indicating that ground state regeneration remains thermodynamically feasible, albeit with varying driving forces compared to the isolated complexes.

In summary, the cyclometalated C^N ligand promotes electron injection by raising the LUMO level but may hinder ground state regeneration by increasing the HOMO level. Conversely, the diimine N^N ligand facilitates ground state regeneration by lowering the HOMO level, while slightly reducing the driving force for electron injection by lowering the LUMO level. The presence of a pyrenyl group increases the LUMO energy level, which may enhance electron injection efficiency, and decreases the HOMO energy level in isolated systems, slightly increasing the driving force for ground state regeneration. The incorporation of heteroatom-containing pyrene derivatives (R1 and R2 groups) further modulates the chemical reactivity, energy alignment, and electron-donating/accepting abilities of the complexes. The attachment of GQD leads to complex effects on the electron injection and ground state regeneration processes, as well as on the chemical hardness, chemical potential, and electron-donating power of the Ru(II)-polypyridyl complexes, depending on the specific complex, the presence of the pyrenyl substituent, R1/R2 groups, and the ligand system (C^N or N^N). These effects are not always consistent across the different complexes, highlighting the intricate interplay between the GQD, the Ru(II) center, and the ligand system.

Fig. 2 shows the significant influence of the π orbitals of the C^N ligand and Ru(II) d orbitals in the HOMO, along with the existence of the LUMO in the π* orbitals of the dppz ligand, in GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-p, GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R2 and GQD/Ru(II)-C^N (see Table 2 for more information). The dynamic configuration indicates that the process of photoexcitation in these nanocomposites includes a MLCT transition and a LLCT transition, which are essential for promoting effective charge separation and electron injection in DSSCs. Yet, the distance between the HOMO (found on the C^N ligand) and the LUMO (found on the dppz ligand) could impede the effectiveness of transferring charges from the GQD to the Ru(II) complex. Conversely, in the case of GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p, GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R2 and GQD/Ru(II)-N^N, the HOMO is predominantly influenced by the GQD, whereas the LUMO is primarily located on the dppz ligand of the Ru(II) complex. Efficient charge transfer from the GQD to the Ru(II) complex is facilitated by this dynamic alignment. When the GQD is photoexcited, electrons from its HOMO can easily move to the LUMO of the dppz ligand, leading to the separation of charges in space. The difference in the position of the HOMO in the C^N-based and N^N-based nanocomposites may be due to the unique electronic characteristics of the ligands. The N^N ligand system shows a higher ability than the C^N ligand system in stabilizing the HOMO level of the Ru(II) complex. The stronger electron-withdrawing properties of the N^N ligand can explain the lower energy of HOMO level of the Ru(II) complex, as shown in Fig. 3 (b) and (d).Therefore, the HOMO is mainly found on the C^N ligand in GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-p, GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R2 and GQD/Ru(II)-C^N, but is mostly affected by the GQD in GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p, GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R2 and GQD/Ru(II)-N^N. In the case of GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R1 and GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R1, the HOMOs are dictated by the R1 group, which is unlike the other systems due to the specific electronic properties of the thiophene rings in the R1 group and their interaction with the rest of the complex. The thiophene rings in the R1 group contain sulfur atoms that are moderately electron-withdrawing due to their electronegativity and inductive effects. The strong electron-withdrawing effect of sulfur atoms in the thiophene rings dominates the electronic characteristics of the HOMO, unlike in other complexes where the HOMO is influenced more by the C^N or N^N ligands or the GQD. In other GQD/Ru(II) systems without the R1 group, the HOMO is influenced by a combination of the C^N or N^N ligands and the GQD. The electron-donating or electron-withdrawing nature of these ligands affects the HOMO localization. For example, in GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-p and GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p, the HOMO is influenced by the pyrenyl group and the GQD, leading to different electronic configurations compared to the R1-substituted complexes.

The pyrenyl group has different effects on the HOMO stability of Ru(II) complexes in N^N-based and C^N-based systems, as well as in their corresponding GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites. In N^N-based systems, the pyrenyl group destabilizes the HOMO (Ru(II)-N^N-p: –5.64 eV vs. Ru(II)-N^N: –6.38 eV), while in C^N-based systems, it has minimal effect on the HOMO stability (Ru(II)-C^N-p: –5.21 eV vs. Ru(II)-C^N: –5.22 eV). This difference can be attributed to the nature of the ligands and their interaction with the pyrenyl group. The N^N ligand system is more electron-withdrawing compared to the C^N ligand system. The electron-rich pyrenyl group may engage in stronger electronic communication with the N^N ligand system, leading to HOMO destabilization. In contrast, the less electron-withdrawing C^N ligand system has weaker electronic communication with the pyrenyl group, resulting in minimal impact on HOMO stability. In both N^N and C^N-based nanocomposites, the pyrenyl group stabilizes the HOMO (GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p: –5.33 eV vs. GQD/Ru(II)-N^N: –5.24 eV; GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-p: –5.28 eV vs. GQD/Ru(II)-C^N: –5.18 eV). This stabilization can be explained by the presence of the GQD. The electron-rich and highly conjugated GQD interacts strongly with the pyrenyl group, leading to HOMO stabilization in both cases. The electronic communication between the GQD and the pyrenyl group may overshadow the differences in the ligand systems (N^N vs. C^N), resulting in a similar stabilizing effect on the HOMO. The attachment of the GQD to the Ru(II) complex may alter the electronic environment and the overall charge distribution in the nanocomposites, which could influence the interaction between the pyrenyl group and the ligand systems, contributing to the stabilizing effect of the pyrenyl group on the HOMO. Additionally, the introduction of R1 and R2 groups into Ru(II) complexes significantly impacts the HOMO stability in both N^N-based and C^N-based systems, as well as in their corresponding GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites. In C^N-based complexes, the incorporation of the R1 group leads to a destabilization of the HOMO, resulting in a higher energy level compared to unsubstituted or pyrenyl-substituted C^N complexes. This effect can be primarily attributed to the electron-rich nature of the pyrene core within the R1 group, which tends to donate electron density to the C^N ligand system. While the sulfur atoms in the thiophene rings of R1 do exhibit electron-withdrawing properties, their influence is insufficient to counteract the electron-donating character of the pyrene core in the context of the C^N ligand environment. Furthermore, the introduction of the R1 group extends the π-conjugation of the C^N system, a factor that typically contributes to raising the HOMO energy level. The cyclometalated nature of C^N ligands, which are generally more electron-rich due to the metal–carbon bond, may also enhance their interaction with the electron-donating portions of the R1 group, further contributing to the HOMO destabilization. Conversely, in N^N-based complexes, the electron-withdrawing sulfur atoms in the thiophene rings of the R1 group exert a stabilizing effect on the HOMO, effectively lowering its energy. This contrasting behavior can be explained by several factors. Firstly, the N^N ligand system is inherently more electron-withdrawing than its C^N counterpart due to the presence of two nitrogen atoms. This characteristic enhances the electron-withdrawing effect of the sulfur atoms in the R1 group's thiophene rings. The result is a synergistic effect where the electron-withdrawing nature of both the N^N ligand and the sulfur atoms in R1 work in concert to stabilize the HOMO. Additionally, the N^N ligand system may interact differently with the R1 group compared to the C^N system, potentially allowing for better orbital overlap with the sulfur atoms, further contributing to the HOMO stabilization. The significant impact of the R1 group on the HOMO, leading to greater stabilization in N^N complexes relative to their unsubstituted or pyrenyl-substituted counterparts, can be attributed to several factors. The interaction between the R1 group and the N^N ligand system appears to be stronger than with the C^N system, resulting in more pronounced effects. The complementary electronic effects of the electron-withdrawing N^N ligand and the sulfur atoms in R1 contribute to a more significant stabilization of the HOMO. In GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R1, the attachment of GQD has a negligible effect on the HOMO. For GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R1, the attachment of GQD enhances the stabilization of the HOMO due to the strong electronic interaction between the GQD and the R1 group. The electron-withdrawing sulfur atoms further stabilize the HOMO, making it lower in energy and primarily localized on the R1 group. On the other hand, in C^N-based nanocomposites, the electron-donating nature of these nitrogen atoms of R2 group destabilizes the HOMO, making it higher in energy. The HOMO is influenced by the electron-donating effects of the R2 group, resulting in a higher-energy HOMO compared to the pyrenyl-substituted C^N systems. In GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R2, the attachment of GQD modifies the electronic environment, but the interaction between the GQD and the R2 group results in a HOMO that is less stabilized compared to the R1-substituted systems. In N^N-based nanocomposites, the R2 group leads to a lower-energy HOMO compared to the unsubstituted or pyrenyl-substituted N^N systems. The R2 group, comprising a pyrene core with fused pyrrole and indole moieties, introduces a unique electronic environment when combined with the N^N ligand system. While pyrrole and indole are typically considered electron-rich systems, their interaction with the inherently electron-withdrawing N^N ligand system can result in unexpected electronic effects. The strong electronic interaction between the electron-rich heterocycles of R2 and the electron-deficient N^N system may lead to a more efficient delocalization of electrons, potentially stabilizing the HOMO. Furthermore, the significant extension of π-conjugation provided by the R2 group, in combination with the N^N ligand, might contribute to this stabilization effect. For GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R2, the attachment of GQD results in a lower-energy HOMO. The synergistic effect between the GQD and the R2 group plays a crucial role in this HOMO stabilization. The nitrogen atoms in the pyrrole and indole moieties of the R2 group, in conjunction with the electron-withdrawing nature of the N^N ligand system, may interact synergistically with the GQD, leading to enhanced stabilization of the HOMO. Furthermore, the attachment of GQD significantly extends the π-conjugation of the system, potentially leading to better delocalization of electrons, which often results in a lowering of the HOMO energy.

The transfer of charges at the interface between GQD and a Ru(II) complex upon photoexcitation is illustrated in Fig. 3. When both GQD and the Ru(II) complex are excited, electrons from their HOMOs move to their LUMOs. For C^N-based nanocomposites, the photoexcited holes in GQD move to the HOMO of the Ru(II) complex (k2) because the Ru(II) complex has a higher HOMO level than GQD. Concurrently, the electrons that are excited by the photo in GQD move to the LUMO of the Ru(II) compound (k1) due to the fact that the Ru(II) compound has lower LUMO levels. Conversely, in nanocomposites based on N^N (except for GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R1), the holes excited by light in the Ru(II) complex move to the HOMO of GQD (k3) due to the GQD having a higher HOMO level. Nevertheless, the excited electrons from GQD still transfer to the LUMO of the Ru(II) complex. Additionally, there is adiabatic charge transfer occurring between GQD and the Ru(II) complex for N^N-based nanocomposites (k4). The charge transfer pathways depicted in Fig. 4 have significant implications for the performance of photoelectric devices that utilize these nanocomposites. In DSSCs, the crucial electron transfer from the GQD to the LUMO of the Ru (II) complex, known as the k1 pathway, is essential for injecting electrons into the TiO2 semiconductor. Additionally, in the N^N-derived nanocomposites, the movement of photoexcited positive charges from the Ru(II) complex to the GQD (referred to as the k3 pathway) can help restore the oxidized dye using the redox electrolyte, a crucial process in the operation of DSSCs.

The absorption spectra depicted in Fig. 4 reveal that the GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites possess wide absorption bands that span the visible region. This feature is extremely beneficial for uses such as dye-sensitized solar panels, making solar energy collection more efficient. The absorption spectra of the GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites show significant differences in comparison to the absorption spectra of the individual GQD and Ru(II) complexes. This disparity suggests the occurrence of strong electronic interactions between the two components upon the formation of the nanocomposites. Furthermore, the GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites show increased absorption intensities in their absorption spectra compared to the individual GQD and Ru(II) complexes. This enhancement implies that the nanocomposites possess improved light-harvesting capabilities. Fig. 5(a)-(d) show that the GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-p, GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R1, GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R2 and GQD/Ru(II)-C^N nanocomposites have comparable absorption profiles, with three main absorption peaks (Smax1, Smax2 and Smax3) around 330 nm, 425 nm and 550 nm. By analyzing the charge difference density (CDD) in supplemental Table S1, it is found that the three main absorption peaks are admixtures of GQD and dye states, with major contributions from either the dye or GQD. Specifically, the first (Smax1) and third (Smax3) peaks are primarily composed of states from the dye, while the second peak (Smax2) is mainly composed of the GQD state. The character of these major transitions includes ligand-centered (LC) and metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT), along with intermolecular charge transfer (ICT) from GQD to the Ru(II) complex. The primary absorption peaks of N^N-based nanocomposites are situated at roughly the same positions as those of C^N-based nanocomposites. However, the GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p, GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R1, and GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R2 nanocomposites (as shown in Fig. 5(e)-(g)) exhibit their lowest absorption peaks extending into the 600–700 nm region. These peaks are attributed to LC, MLCT, and ICT transitions. In particular, the lowest absorption peak originates from the lowest excited state for GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R1. Similarly, the GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p, GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R1 and GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R2 nanocomposites exhibit three main absorption peaks in their spectra, with the lowest absorption peaks occurring below 600 nm, except for GQD/Ru(II)-N^N. This indicates that the N^N-based nanocomposites generally have better light absorption abilities. The GQD/Ru(II)-N^N only has two main absorption peaks, with the first being the primary one. The N^N ligand system often facilitates efficient charge transfer. In this case, it likely leads to a strong charge transfer transition between the GQD and the Ru(II) complex, as indicated by the charge transfer length in Table 3 . This interaction results in a prominent first absorption peak in GQD/Ru(II)-N^N. Adding the pyrenyl group to GQD/Ru(II)-C^N and GQD/Ru(II)-N^N results in a slight increase in absorption peak wavelengths, indicating that the pyrenyl substituent enhances the π-conjugation compared to the versions without substitution.

The primary feature of the S1 state of C^N-based nanocomposite is the LLCT occurring within the Ru(II) complex, with little participation from the GQD part (supplemental Table S1). Conversely, the S1 state of N^N-based nanocomposites is predominantly influenced by ICT originating from the GQD towards the Ru(II) complex. The spatial extent of charge transfer is quantified by the charge transfer length (ΔL). The ΔL values for the GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites range from 2.59 to 12.8 Å. The nanocomposites based on the N^N ligand system exhibit significantly higher ΔL values compared to the nanocomposites based on the C^N ligand system. This difference indicates that the N^N ligand system promotes more efficient charge separation than the C^N ligand system. The larger ΔL values suggest that the photoexcited electrons and holes are more spatially separated in the N^N-based nanocomposites, reducing the likelihood of charge recombination and enhancing the overall charge separation efficiency. The ΔQ values represent the amount of charge transferred between the GQD and Ru(II) components in the nanocomposites. The ΔQ values for the GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites range from –0.069 to 0.97 e−. Notably, the nanocomposites based on the N^N ligand system show much higher ΔQ values compared to the nanocomposites based on the C^N ligand system. The higher ΔQ values in the N^N-based nanocomposites indicate a more effective charge transfer from the GQD to the Ru(II) complex, emphasizing the superiority of the N^N ligand system compared to the C^N ligand system in facilitating interfragment charge transfer. The presence of the R1 and R2 groups also influences the charge transfer dynamics in the nanocomposites. In GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R1 and GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R1, the negative ΔQ values (–0.069 and –0.040 e−, respectively) suggest a reduced forward charge transfer from the GQD to the Ru(II) complex compared to other nanocomposites. This behavior can be attributed to the electron-withdrawing nature of the sulfur atoms in the thiophene rings of the R1 group, which stabilizes the LUMO of the Ru(II) complex. The stabilization of the LUMO reduces the driving force for electron injection from the GQD to the Ru(II) complex, as the energy difference between the LUMO of the GQD and the LUMO of the Ru(II) complex becomes smaller. Consequently, the forward charge transfer is less favorable in these nanocomposites. On the other hand, the positive ΔQ values observed in GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R2 and GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R2 (0.063 and 0.97 e−, respectively) indicate an effective forward charge transfer from the GQD to the Ru(II) complex. The electron-donating nature of the nitrogen atoms in the pyrrole and indole rings of the R2 group destabilizes the LUMO of the Ru(II) complex, increasing the energy difference between the LUMO of the GQD and the LUMO of the Ru(II) complex. This increased energy difference enhances the driving force for electron injection from the GQD to the Ru(II) complex, promoting efficient forward charge transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Changes in free energy from electron injection and ground state regeneration
ce:section.ce:para:
The performance of photovoltaic and optoelectronic devices hinges on the efficient generation, separation, and collection of charge carriers. Two critical factors influence these processes: the driving force of electron injection (ΔGinj), and the driving force of ground state regeneration (ΔGreg). The driving force for electron injection refers to the energy difference between the excited state of the light-absorbing species and the conduction band edge of the electron-accepting material. This difference directly affects the charge injection efficiency, which measures the likelihood of successful electron transfer from the donor to the acceptor. Generally, a larger driving force leads to a higher rate of electron transfer, resulting in increased charge injection efficiency. Ground state regeneration is another crucial process, particularly in DSSCs and similar devices. The driving force for ground state regeneration is the energy difference between the redox potential of the electrolyte (or hole transport material) and the ground state of the light-absorbing species. This parameter greatly influences charge collection efficiency, defined as the probability of collecting an injected charge carrier at the electrode. As the driving force for regeneration increases, the rate of this process also increases, enhancing charge collection efficiency. Achieving optimal device performance involves balancing these competing factors. The ΔGinj and ΔGreg are given by the following equations [61]:

represents the oxidation potential of the light sensitizer in its ground state, approximated by the negative value of the HOMO.

The data in Table 3 shows that the C^N ligand complexes, particularly Ru(II)-C^N-p and Ru(II)-C^N, have a lower ΔGinj value than the N^N ligand complexes. The C^N ligand system is shown to be superior in creating a more powerful driving force for electron injection from the sensitizer's excited state to the TiO2 conduction band, outperforming N^N ligands. On the other hand, the N^N systems exhibit a higher number of negative ΔGreg values compared to the C^N systems, suggesting a stronger thermodynamic push for dye oxidation by the redox couple (I−/I3−) in N^N ligands. The introduction of pyrenyl substituents affects the driving force for electron injection differently depending on the ligand system. For C^N-based dyes, the pyrenyl group slightly increases the driving force, indicated by a more negative ΔGinj value for Ru(II)-C^N-p (–0.86 eV) compared to Ru(II)-C^N (–0.80 eV). This increase is due to the electron-donating nature of the pyrenyl moiety, which raises the LUMO energy level and facilitates electron injection. Conversely, in N^N-based dyes, the pyrenyl group significantly decreases the driving force for electron injection. Ru(II)-N^N-p shows a ΔGinj value of –0.71 eV, much more positive than Ru(II)-N^N's –1.30 eV. The decreased driving force in these dyes can be attributed to the extended π conjugation caused by the pyrenyl group, which lowers the LUMO energy level and impedes electron injection.

The incorporation of R1 and R2 groups, which are heteroatom-containing pyrene derivatives, also significantly impacts the driving forces for electron injection and ground state regeneration. In C^N-based systems, the R1 group enhances the driving force for electron injection, as indicated by the more negative ΔGinj value of Ru(II)-C^N-R1 (–0.92 eV) compared to Ru(II)-C^N (–0.80 eV). The R2 group slightly decreases the driving force for electron injection in C^N-based systems, with Ru(II)-C^N-R2 exhibiting a ΔGinj value of –0.79 eV. Although the electron-donating nitrogen atoms in the R2 group raise the LUMO energy level, which should facilitate the electron injection process, the slight decrease in the driving force suggests that other factors, such as the overall electronic structure and the interaction between the R2 group and the C^N ligand system, may have a more dominant effect on the electron injection process. In N^N-based systems, the R1 group significantly reduces the driving force for electron injection, with Ru(II)-N^N-R1 showing a ΔGinj value of –0.45 eV, which is considerably more positive than that of Ru(II)-N^N (–1.30 eV). The electron-withdrawing sulfur atoms in the R1 group stabilize the HOMO level more than the LUMO, reducing the energy gap and making electron injection less favorable. On the other hand, the R2 group increases the driving force for electron injection in N^N-based systems, with Ru(II)-N^N-R2 exhibiting a ΔGinj value of –0.34 eV. The electron-donating nitrogen atoms in the R2 group raise the LUMO energy level, thus enhancing the electron injection process.

Regarding the impact of R1 and R2 groups on the driving force for ground state regeneration, the effects vary depending on the ligand system. In C^N-based systems, R2 group slightly increase the driving force for ground state regeneration, as indicated by the more negative ΔGreg values of Ru(II)-C^N-R2 (–0.63 eV) compared to Ru(II)-C^N (–0.62 eV). The electron-donating character of the nitrogen atoms in the R2 group lower the HOMO energy level, thus enhancing the oxidation potential of the dye and facilitating ground state regeneration. In contrast, R1 group significantly reduces the driving force of ground state regeneration. In N^N-based systems, the R1 group slightly increases the driving force for ground state regeneration, with Ru(II)-N^N-R1 showing a ΔGreg value of –0.66 eV, which is more negative than that of Ru(II)-N^N (–0.58 eV). On the other hand, the R2 group significantly increases the driving force for ground state regeneration in N^N-based systems, with Ru(II)-N^N-R2 exhibiting a ΔGreg value of –1.09 eV. The electron-donating nitrogen atoms in the R2 group lower the HOMO energy level, thus enhancing the oxidation potential of the dye and promoting ground state regeneration. Regarding the impact of pyrenyl substituents on the driving force for ground state regeneration, the effects vary depending on the ligand system. For C^N-based dyes, the pyrenyl group decreases the driving force for ground state regeneration, as indicated by the less negative ΔGreg value of Ru(II)-C^N-p (–0.58 eV) compared to Ru(II)-C^N (–0.62 eV). This reduction can be attributed to the electron-donating nature of the pyrenyl group, which raises the HOMO energy level and diminishes the oxidation potential of the dye. In contrast, for N^N-based dyes, the pyrenyl group increases the driving force for ground state regeneration. Ru(II)-N^N-p shows a more negative ΔGreg value (–0.68 eV) compared to Ru(II)-N^N (–0.58 eV), suggesting an enhanced thermodynamic favorability for dye regeneration. The opposing effects of the pyrenyl group on ground state regeneration in C^N and N^N-based dyes can be explained by the different electronic characteristics of the ligand systems and their interaction with the pyrenyl moiety.

Highly negative ΔGinj values are important for efficient electron injection in DSSCs. The efficiency of injecting electrons in C^N-based systems, especially Ru(II)-C^N-R1, is significantly enhanced because of its remarkably low ΔGinj value in comparison to N^N-based systems. The improvement in electron injection results in a higher short-circuit current, ultimately leading to increased power conversion efficiencies in the devices. Conversely, adverse ΔGreg values are essential for the effective operation of DSSCs as they help maintain a steady flow of regenerated oxidized dye through the redox couple. N^N systems, particularly Ru(II)-N^N-R2, show a greater ΔGreg than C^N, leading to improved dye regeneration. The prevention of recombination losses is a critical factor in ensuring effective dye regeneration and maintaining a high open circuit voltage in DSSCs.

Connecting GQD to the Ru(II) complex typically results in a decrease in the ΔGinj values for both C^N and N^N-based systems. Yet, for GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R1, the ΔGinj value is slightly lower than that of its standalone form. This indicates that in general, the existence of GQDs could potentially reduce the driving force for electron injection to some extent. However, all GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites continue to show adequately low ΔGinj values, guaranteeing electron injection that is thermodynamically favorable. Conversely, the presence of GQD results in decreased ΔGreg values across all systems, suggesting a diminished incentive for dye regeneration. This effect is more significant for the N^N-based nanocomposites, as the ΔGreg values become significantly less negative when GQDs are present compared to their isolated counterparts. Even though the driving force for dye regeneration is reduced with GQDs present, the negative ΔGreg values still guarantee that the oxidized dye can be regenerated by the redox couple in a thermodynamically feasible manner.

The field of solar energy technology can be revolutionized by materials like the GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites, which have the ability to efficiently convert light energy into electrical energy. This process, known as photoelectric energy conversion, is crucial for the development of high-performance photovoltaic devices. The GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites have wide absorption bands that cover the entire visible spectrum, allowing them to efficiently capture various forms of solar energy. Moreover, the strong interactions between the GQD and ruthenium (Ru(II)) components within the nanocomposites result in a significant enhancement of light absorption intensity across a broad spectrum of wavelengths. The enhancement significantly maximizes the conversion of light particles into electrical energy within solar panels, showcasing the immense potential of these materials.

The energy conversion efficiency (Ω) is obtained by:

where, in the context of power conversion efficiency, denoted by Ω, it is defined as the ratio between the maximum electrical power output (Pout) and the input power of the incident light (Pin). Voc is the maximum voltage that a photovoltaic cell can produce without current, while Jsc is the highest current generated by the cell with zero voltage. The FF quantifies the rectangular shape of the I-V curve by comparing Pmax (i.e., the theoretical maximum power that a photovoltaic cell can generate under ideal conditions) to the product of Voc and Jsc. Lastly, Pin refers to the input power of the incident light, typically measured in watts per square meter (W/m2). The equations and methods used to calculate Voc and FF are described in detail in the referenced paper [54]. The Jsc is given by the following equation:

The equation under consideration incorporates several critical variables: the elementary charge q, with a value of 1.602 × 10-19C; the light-harvesting efficiency of the photovoltaic material, LHE(λ), evaluated at a specific wavelength λ; the quantum efficiency of electron injection from the sensitizer's excited state into the semiconductor's conduction band, denoted as ϕinj; the charge collection efficiency at the electrodes, represented by ϕcollect; and the incident solar photon flux Is(λ) at a particular wavelength λ. These variables collectively describe the performance parameters of a photovoltaic device, often in the context of current generation or overall efficiency.

Important factors to consider include the time required for electrons to move from the excited state of the sensitizer to the conduction band of the semiconductor (tinj) and the relaxation time of the sensitizer's excited state (trelax). When tinj is significantly shorter than trelax, the quantum efficiency of electron injection (φinj) approaches 1, indicating that the excited electrons in the sensitizer are efficiently injected into the semiconductor's conduction band before they can relax back to the ground state. On the other hand, as the ratio of tinj to trelax increases, the quantum efficiency of electron injection decreases, suggesting that a larger portion of the excited electrons are lost through relaxation processes instead of being injected. To achieve high photovoltaic performance, it is ideal to have a small tinj and a large trelax, as this combination results in a quantum efficiency of electron injection close to 1. The Newns-Anderson model is utilized to acquire the tinj, with the supplementary materials offering a comprehensive explanation of its specifics. In the case of GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites, electron injection occurs within the femtosecond range (Table 4 ), while the sensitizer typically takes around 10 picoseconds to relax from the excited state [55,56]. Therefore, the φinj can be considered to be unity.

where ttrans is the time taken for charge transfer from the excited sensitizer state to the semiconductor's conduction band, while trec is the time taken for charge recombination between the sensitizer's excited state and the oxidized redox couple. The details of the trec calculation are provided in the supplemental materials. Efficient charge separation occurs when the charge transfer time is much shorter than the charge recombination time. By ensuring the prompt transfer of excited electrons to the semiconductor, the device's efficiency is increased as it prevents recombination with the oxidized dye. In this study, the trec is in microsecond time scale (Table 4), while the ttrans is typically 5 ps [55]. Hence, it is logical to infer that the charge collection efficiency is also at its maximum. As such, the short circuit current can be rewritten as:
