Therefore, the light correction efficiency of GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposite primarily influences the short circuit current.

The data presented in Table 4 reveal notable differences in the energy conversion efficiency among the various GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites. Firstly, the ligand system (C^N or N^N) plays a crucial role in determining energy conversion efficiency. The C^N-based nanocomposites (GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-p and GQD/Ru(II)-C^N) exhibit superior energy conversion efficiencies (14.9 % and 15.6 %, respectively) compared to their N^N-based counterparts (GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p and GQD/Ru(II)-N^N, with efficiencies of 13.4 % and 7.02 %, respectively). This difference can be attributed to the higher Voc and FF of the C^N-based nanocomposites. The Voc values for GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-p and GQD/Ru(II)-C^N are 1.37 eV and 1.43 eV, respectively, while the FF values are both 0.91. In contrast, the N^N-based nanocomposites have lower Voc values (0.71 eV for GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p and 0.73 eV for GQD/Ru(II)-N^N) and lower FF values (0.85 for both). The higher Voc and FF values of the C^N-based nanocomposites contribute to their enhanced energy conversion efficiencies. Secondly, the presence of the pyrenyl substituent affects the energy conversion efficiency differently depending on the ligand system. In the C^N-based nanocomposites, the presence of the pyrenyl substituent (GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-p) slightly reduces the efficiency (14.9 %) compared to the unsubstituted counterpart (GQD/Ru(II)-C^N, 15.6 %). This reduction can be attributed to the slightly lower Voc value of GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-p (1.37 eV) compared to GQD/Ru(II)-C^N (1.43 eV). On the other hand, in the N^N-based nanocomposites, the presence of the pyrenyl substituent (GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p) significantly enhances the energy conversion efficiency (13.4 %) compared to the unsubstituted counterpart (GQD/Ru(II)-N^N, 7.02 %). This improvement is mainly due to the much higher Jsc of GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p (22.2 mA/cm2) compared to GQD/Ru(II)-N^N (11.3 mA/cm2). The pyrenyl substituent in GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p extends the π-conjugation, leading to increased light absorption and enhanced photocurrent generation. Thirdly, the incorporation of R1 and R2 substituents also influences the energy conversion efficiency of the nanocomposites. The presence of the R1 group in GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R1 leads to lower efficiency (2.34 %) compared to its unsubstituted counterparts (GQD/Ru(II)-C^N, 15.6 %). This reduction can be attributed to the lower Jsc values of the R1-substituted nanocomposites (1.81 mA/cm2) compared to the unsubstituted one (12.0 mA/cm2). The presence of the R2 group also leads to lower efficiencies in both C^N-based (GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R2, 2.61 %) and N^N-based (GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R2, 1.49 %) nanocomposites compared to their unsubstituted counterparts. The lower efficiencies of the R2-substituted nanocomposites can be attributed to their lower Jsc values (2.17 mA/cm2 for GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R2 and 2.78 mA/cm2 for GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R2) and lower Voc values (1.33 eV for GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R2 and 0.64 eV for GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R2).

The observed variations in the photovoltaic performance of the GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites can be further explained by analyzing the underlying factors, such as the charge transfer dynamics, ΔGinj and ΔGreg, and the ΔL and ΔQ between the GQD and Ru(II) components. The N^N-based nanocomposites generally exhibit higher ΔL and ΔQ values compared to the C^N-based nanocomposites (Table 3). For example, GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p and GQD/Ru(II)-N^N have ΔL values of 12.6 Å and 12.8 Å, respectively, and ΔQ values of 0.97 e−, while GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-p and GQD/Ru(II)-C^N have lower ΔL values (3.30 Å and 2.77 Å, respectively) and lower ΔQ values (0.12 e− and 0.18 e−, respectively). The higher ΔL and ΔQ values in the N^N-based nanocomposites suggest more efficient charge separation and enhanced charge transfer from the GQD to the Ru(II) complex, which could lead to reduced recombination losses. However, despite the favorable charge transfer dynamics, the N^N-based nanocomposites exhibit lower overall efficiencies compared to the C^N-based nanocomposites due to their lower Voc and FF values. The ΔGinj and ΔGreg also play a role in the photovoltaic performance of the nanocomposites (Table 3). The C^N-based nanocomposites generally have more negative ΔGinj values compared to the N^N-based nanocomposites, indicating a stronger driving force for electron injection. For example, GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R1 and GQD/Ru(II)-C^N have ΔGinj values of –0.94 eV and –0.86 eV, respectively, while GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p and GQD/Ru(II)-N^N have less negative values (–0.56 eV and –0.53 eV, respectively). The more negative ΔGinj values in the C^N-based nanocomposites contribute to their higher efficiencies by promoting efficient electron injection. On the other hand, the N^N-based nanocomposites generally have more negative ΔGreg values, suggesting a stronger driving force for dye regeneration. However, the impact of ΔGreg on the overall efficiency appears to be less significant compared to other factors, such as Voc and FF.

Several factors contribute to the higher Voc observed in C^N-based nanocomposites compared to N^N-based nanocomposites. Firstly, the trec significantly impacts Voc. Nanocomposites with longer trec values, such as GQD/Ru(II)-C^N and GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R1, exhibit higher Voc values (1.43 eV and 1.42 eV, respectively). This indicates that slower charge recombination is crucial for achieving a high Voc. Secondly, the ΔGinj plays a vital role. Nanocomposites with more negative ΔGinj values, like GQD/Ru(II)-C^N-R1 and GQD/Ru(II)-C^N, tend to have higher Voc values (1.42 eV and 1.43 eV, respectively). This suggests that a larger driving force for electron injection contributes to a higher Voc. Thirdly, the relationship between the ΔGreg and Voc is less straightforward. For instance, GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-R2 has the most negative ΔGreg value (–1.11 eV) but shows the lowest Voc (0.64 eV). This suggests that other factors, such as trec and ΔGinj, have a more dominant influence on Voc. Lastly, the impact of the tinj on Voc is less apparent compared to trec and ΔGinj. Although shorter tinj values generally favor efficient electron injection, the relationship between tinj and Voc is complex. Voc is determined by the interplay of various processes, including charge injection, transport, recombination, and accumulation. Therefore, optimizing tinj alone may not lead to a significant improvement in Voc.

Overall, the arrangement of the GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites greatly influences their ability to convert energy efficiently. The C^N ligand system allows for increased open-circuit voltages, leading to improved energy conversion efficiencies when compared to nanocomposites based on N^N. The impact of the pyrenyl, R1 and R2 substituents vary depending on the ligand system − it leads to a slight reduction in efficiency for the C^N-based nanocomposite, but an improvement in efficiency for the N^N-based nanocomposite.

The nonlinear optical properties of GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites are intimately linked to their photovoltaic performance through several key aspects. Enhanced light absorption in the near-infrared region through two-photon absorption, effective charge generation and separation upon near-infrared photon absorption, improved electron injection and transport facilitated by strong electronic coupling between GQDs and Ru(II) complexes, minimized charge recombination thanks to efficient charge separation and transport at the GQD/Ru(II) interface, and increased photostability due to strong electronic coupling and efficient charge transfer between the components are some of the key factors. These factors synergistically contribute to the overall enhancement of photovoltaic efficiency in devices incorporating GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites. In this study, we utilize total hyperpolarizability (κtot) to describe the nonlinear optical behavior of both isolated Ru(II) complexes and their nanocomposites. The calculation details of κtot are given in supplemental materials.

In this study, we utilized total hyperpolarizability (κtot) as a descriptor for the NLO behavior of both isolated Ru(II) complexes and their GQD-containing nanocomposites. Our findings reveal that the effect of the pyrenyl group on κtot demonstrates a marked dependence on the ligand system. In C^N-based complexes, the addition of the pyrenyl group leads to a decrease in κtot (Ru(II)-C^N-p: 2.58 × 10−2⁶ e.s.u vs Ru(II)-C^N: 1.11 × 10−24 e.s.u; see Table 5 ), whereas in N^N-based systems, it results in an increase (Ru(II)-N^N-p: 4.55 × 10−25 e.s.u vs Ru(II)-N^N: 2.95 × 10−2⁶ e.s.u). This contrasting behavior suggests different interactions between the pyrenyl group and the two ligand systems, possibly related to changes in electron delocalization or polarizability. Furthermore, N^N-based complexes consistently exhibit higher κtot values compared to their C^N counterparts, both in isolated complexes and GQD nanocomposites. This trend can be attributed to the stronger electron-withdrawing nature of the N^N ligand system, which enhances the overall nonlinear optical response.

The incorporation of R1 and R2 groups further modulates the NLO properties, with their effects varying depending on the ligand system. In isolated C^N complexes, both R1 and R2 groups decrease κtot, while in N^N systems, they lead to an increase. This observation highlights the intricate interplay between these substituents and the ligand systems in determining NLO properties, emphasizing the importance of molecular design in tailoring materials for specific NLO applications. The integration of GQDs into the Ru(II) complexes generally results in a significant enhancement of κtot compared to the isolated complexes. This effect is particularly pronounced in N^N-based nanocomposites, with GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p and GQD/Ru(II)-N^N both exhibiting κtot values of 1.17 × 10−23 e.s.u, a substantial increase from their isolated counterparts (Ru(II)-N^N-p: 4.55 × 10−25 e.s.u and Ru(II)-N^N: 2.95 × 10−2⁶ e.s.u). This enhancement can be attributed to the extended π-conjugation provided by the GQDs and the efficient charge transfer between the GQD and Ru(II) components. However, an interesting exception is observed in the case of GQD/Ru(II)-C^N, which shows a smaller κtot (3.48 × 10−25 e.s.u) compared to the isolated Ru(II)-C^N (1.11 × 10−24 e.s.u). This anomaly suggests that in this specific case, the interaction between the GQD and the C^N ligand might disrupt the electronic structure or charge transfer pathways, leading to a reduction in the NLO response.

The largest NLO responses observed in this study are for the GQD/Ru(II)-N^N-p and GQD/Ru(II)-N^N nanocomposites, both with κtot values of 1.17 × 10−23 e.s.u. This observation indicates a synergistic effect between the N^N ligand system, GQD, and potentially the pyrenyl group in enhancing NLO properties. Such significant enhancement of NLO properties in these nanocomposites, particularly in N^N-based systems, suggests their potential use in applications such as optical limiting, two-photon absorption, and nonlinear optical switching.

In this study, we have conducted a comprehensive investigation of the optoelectronic and photoelectric properties of GQD-Ru(II) polypyridyl nanocomposites using DFT calculations. The findings demonstrate that the pyrenyl, 2,8-di-tert-butylpyreno[4,5-b:9,10-b']dithiophene, and 2,8-di-tert-butyl-4,10-dihydropyrrolo[3′,2′:9,10]phenanthro[4,5-efg]indole substituents, as well as the choice of ligand system (C^N or N^N), have a substantial impact on the electronic structures, optical properties, free energy changes associated with electron injection (ΔGinj) and ground state regeneration (ΔGreg), and nonlinear optical characteristics of the nanocomposites.

The electronic structures of GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites are greatly influenced by the ligand system and the substituents. In C^N-based nanocomposites, the spatial separation of the HOMO (located on the C^N ligand) and LUMO (located on the dppz ligand) could hinder the efficiency of charge transfer from the GQD to the Ru(II) complex. Conversely, in N^N-based nanocomposites, the HOMO is predominantly influenced by the GQD, and the LUMO is primarily located on the dppz ligand of the Ru(II) complex, facilitating efficient charge transfer. The optical properties of GQD-Ru(II) nanocomposites are characterized by broad absorption bands spanning the visible region, with the N^N-based nanocomposites generally exhibiting better light absorption abilities. The C^N-based nanocomposites exhibit more negative ΔGinj values, indicating a stronger driving force for electron injection, while the N^N-based nanocomposites show more negative ΔGreg values, suggesting a stronger thermodynamic favorability for dye regeneration. The presence of GQDs generally leads to a decrease in both ΔGinj and ΔGreg values, but the negative values ensure thermodynamically favorable conditions for electron injection and dye regeneration. The C^N-based nanocomposites exhibit superior energy conversion efficiencies (14.9–15.6 %) compared to their N^N-based counterparts (1.49–13.4 %), primarily due to their higher open-circuit voltages (Voc) and fill factors (FF). Several factors contribute to the higher Voc in C^N-based nanocomposites, including longer charge recombination times (trec), more negative ΔGinj values, and the complex interplay between various charge transfer processes. Furthermore, the nanocomposites show much greater nonlinear optical characteristics than the individual Ru(II) complexes, especially the N^N-based nanocomposites, which have significantly higher total hyperpolarizability values.

These findings provide valuable insights for the rational design of advanced materials for photoelectric applications. The results offer guidance for the development of high-performance photovoltaic devices, sensors, and light-emitting devices based on GQD-Ru(II) polypyridyl nanocomposites, ultimately contributing to the advancement of photoelectric technologies and their broader impact on society.
